requirements: The core contributions of 'SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Quantization for Large Language Models' have been reproduced, including the SmoothQuant per-channel smoothing transformation, its integration into PyTorch and FasterTransformer with INT8 kernels, accuracy preservation across OPT/BLOOM/GLM/MT-NLG and other LLMs under W8A8 quantization, and the measured speedup and memory savings.

sub_tasks.requirements: The integer INT8 uniform quantization primitives and granularity levels (per-tensor, per-token, per-channel, group-wise; static vs dynamic) described in Section 2 and Figure 3 have been implemented correctly.

The uniform INT8 quantization formula from Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented: given a floating-point tensor X, the quantized tensor X_INT8 = clip(round(X / Δ), -128, 127) where the scale factor Δ is computed as max(|X|) / 127 (i.e., symmetric quantization with the scale derived from the absolute maximum value of the tensor).

The rounding operation from Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented: given a floating-point tensor X and scale factor Δ, the expression round(X / Δ) is computed using a round-to-nearest function (denoted ⌈·⌋ in the paper), converting the scaled tensor to integer values before clipping.

The round-to-nearest function (denoted ⌈·⌋ in Equation 1 of Section 2) has been implemented: given the scaled floating-point value X / Δ, each element is rounded to the nearest integer using a round-half-up or round-half-to-even convention, producing an integer-valued tensor before any clipping step.

The round-to-nearest convention used in the ⌈·⌋ function of Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented: the rounding mode is explicitly set to either round-half-up (i.e., 0.5 rounds toward positive infinity) or round-half-to-even (banker's rounding, i.e., 0.5 rounds toward the nearest even integer). The chosen convention must be consistently applied to every element of the scaled tensor X / Δ, and the implementation must not silently use a different mode such as round-toward-zero (truncation) or round-half-down.

The round-to-nearest function ⌈·⌋ from Equation 1 (Section 2) is applied element-wise to the already-divided tensor X^FP16 / Δ: every scalar element of the scaled tensor is independently rounded to its nearest integer, producing an integer-valued tensor of the same shape as the input. The rounding must not be applied to X before division, nor to Δ before division, nor to any aggregate statistic — only to each element of the per-element quotient.

The output of the ⌈·⌋ rounding function from Equation 1 (Section 2) is an integer-valued tensor (all elements are whole numbers with no fractional part) that serves as the intermediate result before any clipping to the INT8 representable range [-127, 127] is applied. This integer-valued intermediate tensor must be distinct from the final clipped INT8 tensor: the implementation must not merge rounding and clipping into a single fused operation that skips producing the unclipped integer-valued intermediate, as the two steps are logically separate in the formula X_INT8 = ⌈X / Δ⌋ followed by clip(·, -127, 127).

The division step of the quantization operation from Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented: the floating-point tensor X^FP16 is element-wise divided by the scalar (or per-channel/per-token) scale factor Δ before the rounding function is applied, i.e., the operation is round(X / Δ) and not round(X) / Δ, preserving the correct order of operations.

The scale factor Δ from Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented: Δ = max(|X|) / (2^(N-1) - 1), where N=8 for INT8 quantization, yielding Δ = max(|X|) / 127. This scale is derived from the absolute maximum value of the tensor (symmetric quantization assumed at 0).

The absolute maximum value computation in the scale factor formula from Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented: given a floating-point tensor X, max(|X|) is computed as the maximum of the element-wise absolute values across all elements in the tensor (or across the relevant dimension for per-token/per-channel variants), yielding the global peak magnitude used to derive Δ.

The element-wise absolute value computation in max(|X|) from Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented: for every scalar element x_i in the floating-point tensor X, the absolute value |x_i| is computed independently, producing an intermediate tensor |X| of the same shape as X. This per-element abs operation must be applied before any reduction (max), and must handle negative values, positive values, and zero correctly.

The global maximum reduction in max(|X|) from Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented for the per-tensor case: after computing |X| element-wise, a single scalar maximum is computed over all elements of the entire tensor (regardless of shape or number of dimensions), yielding one scalar peak magnitude value used as the numerator of Δ = max(|X|) / 127. This global reduction collapses all dimensions and must not inadvertently retain a per-row or per-column structure.

The per-token variant of max(|X|) described in Section 2 (Figure 3) has been implemented: for an activation matrix X of shape [T, C_i] (T tokens, C_i input channels), max(|X|) is computed independently for each token row, yielding a vector of T per-token peak magnitudes. Each per-token magnitude serves as the numerator for its own Δ_t = max(|X_t|) / 127, so that each token's quantization scale captures its own dynamic range independently.

The per-channel variant of max(|X|) described in Section 2 (Figure 3) has been implemented for weight tensors: for a weight matrix W of shape [C_i, C_o], max(|W_j|) is computed independently for each input channel j (i.e., each row of W), yielding a vector of C_i per-channel peak magnitudes. Each per-channel magnitude serves as the numerator for its own Δ_j = max(|W_j|) / 127, enabling per-channel weight quantization as used in quantization schemes such as LLM.int8() and SmoothQuant's migration formula (Equation 4).

The denominator of the scale factor formula from Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented with N=8 for INT8 quantization: the expression 2^(N-1) - 1 evaluates to 2^7 - 1 = 127, so that Δ = max(|X|) / 127. The value 127 (not 128 or 255) must be used as the divisor, consistent with the symmetric INT8 range [-127, 127].

The offline (static) mode of scale factor computation described in Section 2 has been implemented: the scale Δ = max(|X|) / 127 is derived from calibration samples drawn from the pre-training dataset (specifically 512 random sentences from the Pile as stated in Section 5.1) and fixed before inference, rather than recomputed per input at runtime. This pre-computed Δ is stored and reused for all downstream evaluation.

The clipping step of the INT8 quantization formula from Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented: after rounding, quantized values are clipped to the representable INT8 range [-127, 127] (symmetric) so that the output tensor X_INT8 contains only valid 8-bit signed integer values.

The lower-bound clipping step of the INT8 quantization formula from Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented: after rounding, any quantized value less than -127 is clamped to -127, enforcing the minimum representable value of the symmetric INT8 range. This ensures that large negative outliers (whose rounded value would fall below -127) are correctly saturated rather than wrapping or causing undefined behavior.

The clamping logic for the lower bound of the symmetric INT8 range has been implemented: after the rounding step round(X / Δ) from Equation 1 (Section 2), every element of the integer-valued intermediate tensor that is strictly less than -127 is replaced with -127. This saturation must be applied before any dtype cast, and must handle the case where large negative activation outliers produce rounded values well below -127 (e.g., -200, -500) by mapping them all to exactly -127.

The lower clipping bound used in the INT8 quantization implementation from Equation 1 (Section 2) is -127, not -128: the paper uses the symmetric INT8 range [-127, 127] derived from 2^(N-1)-1 = 127, so the minimum representable value is -127. The implementation must explicitly use -127 as the lower clamp value and not -128 (which would correspond to the asymmetric INT8 range). Using -128 would violate the symmetric quantization assumption stated in Section 2.

The lower-bound clamping step from Equation 1 (Section 2) ensures that large negative outlier values do not cause integer wrapping, overflow, or undefined behavior when the result is subsequently cast to a signed 8-bit integer dtype: the clamp to -127 is applied while the tensor is still in a wider integer or floating-point representation, so that every value in the tensor is guaranteed to be in [-127, 127] before the final int8 cast. The implementation must not rely on implicit truncation or hardware-defined overflow behavior to handle out-of-range negative values.

The upper-bound clipping step of the INT8 quantization formula from Equation 1 (Section 2) has been implemented: after rounding, any quantized value greater than +127 is clamped to +127, enforcing the maximum representable value of the symmetric INT8 range. Because Δ is derived from max(|X|), values should not normally exceed 127 under symmetric quantization, but the clamp must still be present to handle numerical edge cases (e.g., floating-point rounding of the division).

The clamping logic for the upper bound of the symmetric INT8 range has been implemented: after the rounding step round(X / Δ) from Equation 1 (Section 2), every element of the integer-valued intermediate tensor that is strictly greater than +127 is replaced with +127. This saturation must be applied before any dtype cast, and must correctly handle any numerical edge case (e.g., floating-point rounding errors in the division X / Δ) that could produce a rounded value of 128 or higher.

The upper clipping bound used in the INT8 quantization implementation from Equation 1 (Section 2) is +127, not +128 or +255: the paper uses the symmetric INT8 range [-127, 127] derived from 2^(N-1)-1 = 127, so the maximum representable value is +127. The implementation must explicitly use +127 as the upper clamp value and not a larger value such as +128 (which would exceed the 8-bit signed integer range) or +255 (which would correspond to an unsigned INT8 type).

The upper-bound clamping step from Equation 1 (Section 2) ensures that any value exceeding +127 — however unlikely under a max-derived Δ — does not cause integer wrapping, overflow, or undefined behavior when the result is subsequently cast to a signed 8-bit integer dtype: the clamp to +127 is applied while the tensor is still in a wider integer or floating-point representation so that every value in the tensor is guaranteed to be in [-127, 127] before the final int8 cast. The implementation must not rely on implicit truncation or hardware-defined overflow behavior to handle out-of-range positive values.

After the clip operation described in Section 2, the output tensor X_INT8 is cast to a signed 8-bit integer dtype (e.g., torch.int8 or equivalent), so that the resulting tensor occupies 1 byte per element and is directly consumable by INT8 GEMM kernels (e.g., CUTLASS INT8 GEMM as mentioned in Section 5.1). Storing the clipped values in a wider dtype (e.g., int32 or float32) does not satisfy this requirement.

The symmetric quantization assumption from Section 2 has been implemented as the default: the quantizer assumes the tensor is symmetric about 0 (no zero-point offset), consistent with Equation 1. The implementation must also support the asymmetric case (e.g., after ReLU) by optionally adding a zero-point as described in Section 2 (citing Jacob et al., 2018).

The outlier-preserving property described in Section 2 has been verified in the implementation: because Δ is derived from the global maximum absolute value of the tensor, large outlier values are preserved without clipping under symmetric quantization, consistent with the paper's statement that 'such quantizer uses the maximum absolute value to calculate Δ so that it preserves the outliers in activation, which are found to be important for accuracy.'

Because Δ is derived from max(|X|), no activation value is clipped or saturated during the symmetric quantization process described in Section 2: the largest absolute value in the tensor maps exactly to ±127, and all smaller values map proportionally within [-127, 127]. The implementation must verify (e.g., via assertion or unit test) that for any test tensor, round(X / Δ) produces no value outside [-127, 127] prior to the clipping step, confirming that outliers are preserved rather than truncated.

The largest absolute value in any test tensor maps exactly to ±127 after the symmetric quantization formula from Equation 1 (Section 2): given Δ = max(|X|) / 127, the element x_max = max(|X|) satisfies round(x_max / Δ) = round(127.0) = 127, and the element x_min = -max(|X|) satisfies round(x_min / Δ) = -127. The implementation must include a unit test or assertion verifying this boundary condition for at least one test tensor, confirming that the maximum-magnitude value is not saturated but maps exactly to the INT8 boundary.

The largest positive value in any test tensor, x_max = max(|X|), maps exactly to +127 after the symmetric quantization formula from Equation 1 (Section 2): given Δ = max(|X|) / 127, round(x_max / Δ) = round(127.0) = 127.

The most negative value in any test tensor, x_min = -max(|X|), maps exactly to -127 after the symmetric quantization formula from Equation 1 (Section 2): given Δ = max(|X|) / 127, round(x_min / Δ) = round(-127.0) = -127.
The implementation includes a unit test or assertion verifying the boundary condition that the maximum-magnitude element maps exactly to ±127 (not saturated) for at least one test tensor.

All non-outlier elements of any test tensor X map proportionally within [-127, 127] under the symmetric quantization formula from Equation 1 (Section 2): for a non-outlier element x_i with |x_i| << max(|X|), the rounded value round(x_i / Δ) is a small integer whose magnitude is strictly less than 127 and is proportional to x_i / max(|X|) * 127. A unit test or assertion must confirm that no non-outlier element produces a rounded value outside [-127, 127], consistent with the paper's statement that the max-based Δ preserves outliers at the cost of reducing effective quantization levels for non-outlier channels.

The low effective quantization bits phenomenon described in Section 3 has been verified in the implementation context: for a per-tensor quantized activation matrix where channel i has maximum magnitude m_i and the global maximum is m, the effective quantization levels for channel i is 2^8 * m_i / m. For non-outlier channels where m_i << m, this yields very few effective levels (approximately 2–3 as stated in Section 3), confirming why preserving outliers via a max-derived Δ is important for accuracy despite penalizing non-outlier channels.

The paper's claim from Section 2 that outliers in activations 'are found to be important for accuracy' is corroborated by the implementation: the max-based Δ ensures outlier values are not discarded. This is consistent with the finding from Section 3 and Table 1 that per-tensor INT8 quantization (which preserves outliers in the scale but coarsely quantizes non-outlier channels) still yields very low accuracy (~32–40% average on OPT), demonstrating that while outlier preservation is necessary, it is not sufficient alone — motivating SmoothQuant's migration approach.
The corresponding dequantization formula from Equation 2 (Section 2) has been implemented: X_FP16 ≈ X_INT8 × Δ, converting INT8 activations or weights back to FP16 (or BF16) after the INT8 GEMM.

Per-tensor quantization (Figure 3, Section 2) has been implemented: a single scalar scale factor Δ is computed from the global maximum absolute value across the entire weight or activation tensor, and all elements are quantized with this single scale.

Per-token quantization for activations (Figure 3, Section 2) has been implemented: a separate scale factor Δ_i is computed per output token (i.e., per row of the activation matrix), so that each token's dynamic range is captured independently.

Per-channel quantization for weights (Figure 3, Section 2) has been implemented: a separate scale factor Δ_j is computed per output channel (i.e., per column/row of the weight matrix depending on convention), so that each channel's dynamic range is captured independently.

Both static and dynamic scale calibration modes described in Section 2 have been implemented: in static mode the scale Δ is computed offline from a calibration dataset and fixed at inference time, while in dynamic mode the scale Δ is computed online from each input activation tensor at runtime.
The analysis of activation quantization difficulty from Section 3, including the simulated per-channel activation quantization experiment reproducing Table 1, has been reproduced.

Code exists to visualize the input activations and weights of a linear layer that exhibits large quantization error, reproducing Figure 4 (left) from Section 3. The visualization must show that the weight distribution is uniform and flat (easy to quantize), while the activation distribution contains a small number of outlier channels whose magnitudes are approximately 100× larger than most activation values. The outlier channels must be visually distinguishable (e.g., highlighted in red) from normal channels.

Code exists to visualize the weight matrix of the selected OPT linear layer (one with large quantization error), showing that the weight distribution is uniform and flat with no large outliers, consistent with Figure 4 (left) of Section 3.

Code exists to visualize the input activation matrix of the selected OPT linear layer, showing that a small number of outlier channels have magnitudes approximately 100× larger than most activation values, with outlier channels visually distinguishable (e.g., highlighted in red) from normal channels, consistent with Figure 4 (left) of Section 3.

The outlier persistence property described in Section 3 has been verified empirically: for a selected linear layer from an OPT model, it is shown that if a channel has an outlier for one token, the outlier persistently appears across all tokens (low variance between magnitudes of a given channel across tokens), while the variance between channels for a given token is large. This corresponds to the red-highlighted channels in Figure 4 of Section 3.

It has been empirically verified that for the selected OPT linear layer, the variance of activation magnitudes for a given outlier channel across all tokens is low: the maximum magnitude of each outlier channel (highlighted in red in Figure 4 of Section 3) remains consistently large across all token positions in the input sequence, confirming the paper's statement that 'if one channel has an outlier, it persistently appears in all tokens.'

It has been empirically verified that for the selected OPT linear layer, the variance of activation magnitudes across channels for any given token is large: some channels (outlier channels) have magnitudes approximately 100× larger than the majority of non-outlier channels within the same token row, consistent with the statement in Section 3 that 'the variance amongst the channels for a given token is large (the activations in some channels are very large, but most are small).'

A specific linear layer from an OPT model that exhibits large quantization error has been selected, and the activation matrix X of shape [T, C_i] for that layer has been extracted using calibration samples. At least one outlier channel (a column j of X) has been identified such that max(|X_j|) is approximately 100× larger than the median or majority of other channel maxima, consistent with Figure 4 (left) of Section 3. The selection and extraction code must be runnable and produce the activation matrix used in the subsequent verification steps.

It has been empirically verified that for the selected OPT linear layer, the variance of activation magnitudes across channels for any given token row is large: for a representative sample of token rows from the extracted activation matrix X, the coefficient of variation (std / mean) of |X[t, :]| across all C_i channels is computed and shown to be substantially large (reflecting that some channel values are very large while most are small). This must be reported as a scalar or plotted for multiple token rows, confirming the paper's statement in Section 3 that 'the variance amongst the channels for a given token is large.'

As a quantitative contrast, it has been verified that the inter-token variance (variance of |X_j| across T token positions for a given outlier channel j) is substantially smaller than the inter-channel variance (variance of |X[t, :]| across C_i channels for a given token t): the coefficient of variation of |X_j| across tokens for the outlier channel is computed and shown to be much smaller than the coefficient of variation across channels for any single token row. This contrast confirms both halves of Section 3's statement: outlier channels are 'consistently large' across tokens AND the variance across channels for a given token is large.

A visualization reproducing Figure 4 (left panel) from Section 3 has been generated: the input activation matrix of the selected OPT linear layer is shown with outlier channels highlighted in red, visually demonstrating that the same channels are consistently large across all token rows (low inter-token variance per channel) while non-outlier channels remain small, contrasted against the uniform and flat weight distribution that is easy to quantize.

A visualization of the input activation matrix of the selected OPT linear layer has been generated showing outlier channels highlighted in red (as in Figure 4 left panel, Section 3): the activation matrix of shape [T, C_i] is displayed (e.g., as a heatmap or channel-wise magnitude bar chart), with each column corresponding to an input channel, and outlier channels — those whose maximum magnitude across all token rows is approximately 100× larger than non-outlier channels — are visually distinguished in red. The visualization must make it immediately clear which specific channels are outliers and which are not.

The visualization reproducing Figure 4 (left panel, Section 3) includes a contrasting display of the weight matrix W of the same OPT linear layer, showing that the weight distribution is uniform and flat with no large outliers: the weight magnitude heatmap or bar chart must not contain any channels with magnitudes dramatically larger than the rest, consistent with Section 3's statement that 'the weight distribution is quite uniform and flat, which is easy to quantize.' The side-by-side or paired presentation of the activation (with red outliers) and the weight (uniform) makes the contrast visually explicit.

Simulated per-channel activation quantization has been implemented for the INT8 quantization experiments in Table 1: for an activation matrix X of shape [T, C_i], a separate scale factor Δ_j = max(|X_j|) / 127 is computed per input channel j (i.e., per column of X), so that each channel's own dynamic range is captured independently. This is distinct from per-token (per-row) and per-tensor quantization.

For the simulated per-channel activation quantization experiment (Table 1, Section 3), the scale factor computation has been implemented such that for an activation matrix X of shape [T, C_i], a separate scalar scale Δ_j = max(|X_j|) / 127 is computed independently for each input channel j (i.e., each column j of X across all T token rows), yielding a vector of C_i per-channel scale factors. The max reduction for channel j collapses only the T token dimension, not the channel dimension, so each channel's own peak magnitude determines its own scale.

The simulated per-channel activation quantization used in Table 1 (Section 3) is explicitly distinct from per-token quantization in the implementation: per-token quantization computes one scale per row (token) of X, collapsing the C_i channel dimension, whereas per-channel quantization computes one scale per column (channel) of X, collapsing the T token dimension. The implementation must demonstrate both modes separately and confirm that the two produce different scale vectors — a vector of length T for per-token and a vector of length C_i for per-channel — on the same test activation matrix.

The implementation correctly reflects the hardware incompatibility of per-channel activation quantization noted in Section 3 and Table 1: per-channel activation scaling requires a per-column (inner dimension) rescaling of X that cannot be folded into the outer-dimension rescaling of Equation 2 (Section 3), which only allows scaling along the token dimension T of activations and the output channel dimension C_o of weights after the INT8 GEMM finishes. The code must include a comment, assertion, or documentation note marking per-channel activation quantization as simulation-only (not runnable with INT8 GEMM kernels), consistent with the gray marking in Table 1.

The evaluation metric used in Table 1 has been implemented: the reported accuracy is the average over four zero-shot tasks — WinoGrande, HellaSwag, PIQA, and LAMBADA — evaluated on OPT models under each quantization scheme (FP16, INT8 per-tensor, INT8 per-token, INT8 per-channel). The evaluation harness used is consistent with Section 5.1 of the paper (lm-evaluation-harness).

The averaging logic for the Table 1 evaluation metric has been implemented: given per-task accuracy scores for WinoGrande, HellaSwag, PIQA, and LAMBADA, the reported accuracy is the arithmetic mean of exactly these four scores (not a subset, not a superset), producing a single scalar percentage value that is directly comparable to the values in Table 1.

The WinoGrande zero-shot evaluation task has been configured and run using lm-evaluation-harness (as specified in Section 5.1): the model is evaluated on the WinoGrande dataset in a zero-shot setting, producing an accuracy score that is one of the four components averaged in Table 1. The harness version and task name must be consistent with the paper's setup.

The HellaSwag zero-shot evaluation task has been configured and run using lm-evaluation-harness (as specified in Section 5.1): the model is evaluated on the HellaSwag dataset in a zero-shot setting, producing an accuracy score that is one of the four components averaged in Table 1.

The PIQA zero-shot evaluation task has been configured and run using lm-evaluation-harness (as specified in Section 5.1): the model is evaluated on the PIQA dataset in a zero-shot setting, producing an accuracy score that is one of the four components averaged in Table 1.

The LAMBADA zero-shot evaluation task has been configured and run using lm-evaluation-harness (as specified in Section 5.1): the model is evaluated on the LAMBADA dataset in a zero-shot setting, producing an accuracy score that is one of the four components averaged in Table 1.

The lm-evaluation-harness framework (referenced in Section 5.1 via footnote) has been installed and integrated as the evaluation backend for OPT and BLOOM models: the harness is used to run all four zero-shot tasks (WinoGrande, HellaSwag, PIQA, LAMBADA) and return per-task accuracy values that are then averaged for Table 1. The integration must support both FP16 and INT8-quantized model variants (per-tensor, per-token, per-channel) as inputs to the harness.

OPT models at all five sizes evaluated in Table 1 — OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, OPT-30B, OPT-66B, and OPT-175B — have been loaded or made accessible for evaluation. Each model must be loadable in both FP16 (baseline) and INT8-quantized variants (per-tensor, per-token, per-channel) so that lm-evaluation-harness can run the four zero-shot tasks on each configuration.

The SmoothQuant per-channel smoothing transformation, migration strength alpha formula, offline scale fusion, and the transformer-block precision mapping have been implemented as described in Section 4.

The per-channel smoothing transformation from Equation 3 (Section 4) has been implemented: given activation X and weight W, the smoothed activation X_hat = X * diag(s)^{-1} and smoothed weight W_hat = diag(s) * W are computed, such that Y = X_hat * W_hat = X * W exactly (mathematically equivalent transformation). The smoothing vector s is of dimension C_i (one scale per input channel).

The smoothed activation computation X_hat = X * diag(s)^{-1} from Equation 3 (Section 4) has been implemented: given the activation matrix X of shape [T, C_i] and the per-channel smoothing vector s of dimension C_i, each column j of X is divided element-wise by s_j (i.e., X_hat[:, j] = X[:, j] / s_j for all j = 1..C_i), producing the smoothed activation X_hat of the same shape [T, C_i]. The division must be applied column-wise (per input channel), not row-wise or globally.

The implementation validates that the activation matrix X has shape [T, C_i] and the smoothing vector s has length C_i before performing the division: an explicit assertion or shape check confirms that X.shape[1] == s.shape[0] == C_i, ensuring that the per-channel division is dimensionally consistent and that s is not inadvertently broadcast along the token dimension T.

The column-wise (per-input-channel) division X_hat[:, j] = X[:, j] / s_j from Equation 3 (Section 4) has been implemented for all j = 1..C_i: the implementation divides each column j of X by the scalar s_j, producing the smoothed activation matrix X_hat of the same shape [T, C_i]. The division must collapse across the token dimension T (all T rows of column j share the same divisor s_j) and must NOT use a single global scalar, a per-token (per-row) divisor, or any other axis of reduction.

The output of the smoothed activation computation X_hat = X * diag(s)^{-1} from Equation 3 (Section 4) has the same shape [T, C_i] as the input activation matrix X: after column-wise division by s, the number of tokens T and the number of input channels C_i are unchanged, and no dimension is reduced, transposed, or broadcast away. A unit test confirms that X_hat.shape == X.shape for representative inputs.

The implementation explicitly does NOT apply the smoothing division row-wise (per-token) or globally (per-tensor): the smoothing vector s is applied along the channel axis (axis=1 for a [T, C_i] matrix), not along the token axis (axis=0) and not as a single scalar broadcast over the entire matrix. A unit test confirms that using a smoothing vector s with varying channel magnitudes produces different column-wise scales, i.e., X_hat[:, j1] / X[:, j1] != X_hat[:, j2] / X[:, j2] whenever s_j1 != s_j2, ruling out per-row or per-tensor division.

The smoothed weight computation W_hat = diag(s) * W from Equation 3 (Section 4) has been implemented: given the weight matrix W of shape [C_i, C_o] and the per-channel smoothing vector s of dimension C_i, each row j of W is multiplied element-wise by s_j (i.e., W_hat[j, :] = s_j * W[j, :] for all j = 1..C_i), producing the smoothed weight W_hat of the same shape [C_i, C_o]. This reverses the activation scaling so that the product X_hat * W_hat = X * W remains mathematically equivalent.

The implementation validates that the weight matrix W has shape [C_i, C_o] and the smoothing vector s has length C_i before performing the row-wise multiplication: an explicit assertion or shape check confirms that W.shape[0] == s.shape[0] == C_i, ensuring that the per-channel multiplication is dimensionally consistent and that s is not inadvertently broadcast along the output channel dimension C_o.

The row-wise (per-input-channel) multiplication W_hat[j, :] = s_j * W[j, :] from Equation 3 (Section 4) has been implemented for all j = 1..C_i: for each row j of W (corresponding to input channel j), every element of that row is multiplied by the scalar s_j, producing the smoothed weight matrix W_hat of the same shape [C_i, C_o]. The multiplication must be applied row-wise (along axis=0 of W, collapsing across the C_o output channel dimension) and must NOT use a single global scalar, a per-output-channel (per-column) multiplier, or any other axis of reduction.

The output of the smoothed weight computation W_hat = diag(s) * W from Equation 3 (Section 4) has the same shape [C_i, C_o] as the input weight matrix W: after row-wise multiplication by s, the number of input channels C_i and output channels C_o are unchanged, and no dimension is reduced, transposed, or broadcast away. A unit test confirms that W_hat.shape == W.shape for representative inputs.

The implementation explicitly does NOT apply the smoothing multiplication column-wise (per-output-channel) or globally (per-tensor): the smoothing vector s is applied along the input channel axis (axis=0 for a [C_i, C_o] weight matrix), not along the output channel axis (axis=1) and not as a single scalar broadcast over the entire matrix. A unit test confirms that using a smoothing vector s with varying per-channel magnitudes produces different row-wise scales, i.e., W_hat[j1, :] / W[j1, :] != W_hat[j2, :] / W[j2, :] whenever s_j1 != s_j2, ruling out per-column or per-tensor multiplication.

The smoothed weight W_hat = diag(s) * W is computed offline (i.e., before inference) and stored as the model's updated weight parameters, consistent with Section 4's description that 'we can easily fuse the smoothing factor into previous layers' parameters offline.' The row-wise multiplication by s is performed once during model preparation and the result is saved in place of the original weight W, so that no additional per-inference scaling operation is needed on the weight side during forward passes.

The mathematical equivalence of the SmoothQuant transformation from Equation 3 (Section 4) has been verified: for a test linear layer with input X and weight W, the output Y = X * W equals X_hat * W_hat = (X * diag(s)^{-1}) * (diag(s) * W) to within floating-point precision. A unit test or assertion must confirm that the maximum absolute difference between Y and X_hat * W_hat is negligibly small (e.g., < 1e-5) for representative test inputs with varying outlier channel magnitudes.

The smoothing vector s used in Equation 3 (Section 4) has dimension C_i (one scale factor per input channel of the linear layer): s is a 1-D tensor of length C_i, where C_i is the number of input channels of the weight matrix W of shape [C_i, C_o]. The implementation must confirm that len(s) == W.shape[0] == X.shape[-1], ensuring that each input channel j has exactly one corresponding smoothing factor s_j and that the per-channel scaling is not inadvertently applied along the token dimension T or the output channel dimension C_o.

The migration strength alpha formula from Equation 4 (Section 4) has been implemented: s_j = max(|X_j|)^alpha / max(|W_j|)^(1 - alpha), where j indexes the input channel, alpha is a scalar hyper-parameter in [0, 1], max(|X_j|) is the per-channel maximum absolute value of activations estimated from calibration samples, and max(|W_j|) is the per-channel maximum absolute value of weights. The formula must produce a per-channel smoothing vector s of dimension C_i.

The formula s_j = max(|X_j|)^alpha / max(|W_j|)^(1 - alpha) from Equation 4 (Section 4) correctly indexes by input channel j, where j = 1, 2, ..., C_i. The implementation iterates over or vectorizes across all C_i input channels independently, producing one smoothing scalar s_j per channel, and the resulting smoothing vector s has exactly C_i elements matching the input channel dimension of both X (shape [T, C_i]) and W (shape [C_i, C_o]).

The per-channel maximum absolute activation value max(|X_j|) in Equation 4 (Section 4) has been computed correctly for each input channel j: for an activation matrix X of shape [T, C_i], max(|X_j|) = max over all t in {1..T} of |X[t, j]|, yielding a vector of C_i scalars. This per-column max must be estimated from calibration samples (512 random sentences from the Pile as stated in Section 5.1) rather than from runtime inputs, and the estimated values must be stored offline.

The per-channel maximum absolute weight value max(|W_j|) in Equation 4 (Section 4) has been computed correctly for each input channel j: for a weight matrix W of shape [C_i, C_o], max(|W_j|) = max over all k in {1..C_o} of |W[j, k]|, yielding a vector of C_i scalars. This per-row max is computed directly from the model's stored weight parameters (not from calibration samples) and is fixed for the lifetime of the model.

The activation numerator term max(|X_j|)^alpha in Equation 4 (Section 4) has been implemented: the per-channel activation max vector (of length C_i) is raised element-wise to the power alpha, where alpha is a scalar in [0, 1]. When alpha = 0 the numerator collapses to 1 (no activation contribution), and when alpha = 1 it equals the raw per-channel activation max. The implementation must handle the boundary cases alpha = 0 and alpha = 1 correctly without numerical errors.

The weight denominator term max(|W_j|)^(1 - alpha) in Equation 4 (Section 4) has been implemented: the per-channel weight max vector (of length C_i) is raised element-wise to the power (1 - alpha), where alpha is a scalar in [0, 1]. When alpha = 1 the denominator collapses to 1 (no weight contribution), and when alpha = 0 it equals the raw per-channel weight max. The implementation must handle the boundary cases alpha = 0 and alpha = 1 correctly without numerical errors.

The final per-channel smoothing vector s is produced by element-wise division of the activation numerator by the weight denominator as in Equation 4 (Section 4): s_j = max(|X_j|)^alpha / max(|W_j|)^(1 - alpha) for each j = 1..C_i. The implementation must guard against division by zero (e.g., by clamping max(|W_j|) to a small epsilon) and produce a positive-valued vector s of length C_i that is subsequently passed to the per-channel smoothing transformation in Equation 3.

The migration strength alpha is implemented as a configurable scalar hyper-parameter constrained to [0, 1] as described in Section 4. The implementation accepts alpha as a user-specified argument and validates that 0 <= alpha <= 1. The default values used are alpha = 0.5 for OPT and BLOOM models, alpha = 0.75 for GLM-130B, and alpha = 0.8 for LLaMA-1 models (as stated in Sections 4, 5.1, and Table 6 respectively), and these defaults are set per-model in the configuration.

The output smoothing vector s produced by the Equation 4 formula has been verified to have exactly dimension C_i (one scalar per input channel): the implementation includes an explicit assertion or shape check confirming that s.shape == (C_i,), where C_i equals both X.shape[-1] and W.shape[0]. This ensures that when s is subsequently used in the per-channel smoothing transformation (Equation 3), the column-wise division X / s and row-wise multiplication W * s are dimensionally consistent.

The behavior of the Equation 4 formula at extreme alpha values has been verified, consistent with Section 4's discussion and Figure 10: when alpha is too small (< 0.4), the activation term max(|X_j|)^alpha approaches 1 while the weight denominator max(|W_j|)^(1-alpha) remains large, making weights hard to quantize; when alpha is too large (> 0.6), the activation term dominates and activations remain hard to quantize. The implementation correctly computes s_j under these extreme settings and the unit test confirms that the sweet-spot range 0.4-0.6 yields the most balanced per-channel magnitudes for weights and activations on OPT-175B.

When alpha is too small (< 0.4), the implementation correctly computes s_j such that the activation term max(|X_j|)^alpha approaches 1 while the weight denominator max(|W_j|)^(1-alpha) remains large, making weights hard to quantize, consistent with Section 5.5 and Figure 10.

When alpha is too large (> 0.6), the activation term dominates and activations remain hard to quantize; the implementation correctly computes s_j under these extreme settings, consistent with Section 5.5 and Figure 10.

The sweet-spot range 0.4-0.6 for alpha yields the most balanced per-channel magnitudes for weights and activations on OPT-175B, and a unit test or verification confirms this, consistent with Section 5.5 and Figure 10.

The offline calibration procedure from Section 5.1 has been implemented: activation statistics (per-channel max magnitudes max(|X_j|) for j = 1..C_i) are estimated using 512 random sentences sampled from the pre-training dataset Pile. The same smoothed and quantized model derived from this calibration is then used for all downstream tasks without recomputation.

The offline scale fusion described in Section 4 has been implemented: the per-channel smoothing factor s is fused offline into the parameters of the preceding linear operation (e.g., linear layer or layer norm that produces X) by absorbing diag(s)^{-1} into those parameters, so that no additional kernel call is required at inference time for the activation division. When the input X comes from a residual addition (not a linear layer), an extra explicit scaling on the residual branch is added as described in Section 4.

The transformer-block quantization flow described in Section 4 and shown in Figure 6 has been implemented: INT8 quantization is applied to the inputs and weights of all compute-heavy linear layers (self-attention Q/K/V projections, output projection, and FFN linear layers) and BMM operators in attention, while lightweight element-wise operations (ReLU, Softmax, LayerNorm) keep their activations in FP16. The quantized linear layers use W8A8 with CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels as described in Section 5.1.

The Q, K, and V projection linear layers in the self-attention block have been replaced with INT8 W8A8 linear modules using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels, as described in Section 4 and Figure 6. Both the input activations (after SmoothQuant smoothing) and the weight matrices of these three projection layers are quantized to INT8 before the matrix multiplication, and the output is dequantized to FP16.

The attention output projection linear layer (the linear layer that projects the concatenated multi-head attention outputs back to the hidden dimension) has been replaced with an INT8 W8A8 linear module using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels, consistent with Figure 6 and Section 4. Both the smoothed input activations and the weight matrix of this layer are quantized to INT8 before the GEMM, and the result is dequantized to FP16.

All linear layers in the feed-forward network (FFN) sub-block of each transformer layer — including the up-projection (gate/up) and down-projection layers — have been replaced with INT8 W8A8 linear modules using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels, as described in Section 4 and Figure 6. Both the smoothed input activations and the weight matrices of these layers are quantized to INT8 before the GEMM.

The batched matrix multiplication (BMM) computing attention scores — i.e., the product Q * K^T in the attention mechanism — has been implemented using an INT8 BMM kernel (CUTLASS INT8 GEMM) as described in Section 4 (Figure 6) and Section 5.1. Both the Q and K tensors are quantized to INT8 before this BMM, and the resulting attention score matrix is dequantized to FP16 before the Softmax operation.

The batched matrix multiplication (BMM) computing the attention context — i.e., the product of the attention weights (post-Softmax) with the V matrix — has been implemented using an INT8 BMM kernel (CUTLASS INT8 GEMM) as described in Section 4 (Figure 6) and Section 5.1. The attention weight tensor (re-quantized to INT8 after Softmax in FP16) and the V tensor (quantized to INT8) are multiplied using the INT8 kernel, and the output context is dequantized to FP16.

The Softmax operation in the attention computation remains in FP16 and is NOT quantized to INT8, consistent with Figure 6 and Section 4's design principle of keeping lightweight element-wise operations in FP16. The input to Softmax (the INT8 BMM output of Q*K^T) is dequantized to FP16 before Softmax is applied, and the Softmax output is in FP16. If the subsequent context BMM requires INT8 input, the Softmax FP16 output is re-quantized to INT8 at the boundary.

The LayerNorm operations in each transformer block (both the pre-attention LayerNorm and the pre-FFN LayerNorm) remain in FP16 and are NOT quantized to INT8, consistent with Figure 6 and Section 4. The output of LayerNorm is in FP16, and the per-channel smoothing factor s is fused offline into LayerNorm's learnable scale/bias parameters so that the smoothed activation X_hat is produced in FP16 without an extra kernel call, before being quantized to INT8 at the boundary of the subsequent INT8 linear layer.

All lightweight element-wise activation functions such as ReLU (and analogous non-linearities in the FFN, e.g., GELU or SiLU depending on model architecture) remain in FP16 and are NOT quantized to INT8, consistent with Figure 6 and Section 4's design principle. The output of the FFN's first INT8 linear layer is dequantized to FP16 before the non-linearity, and the non-linearity output is re-quantized to INT8 at the input boundary of the second FFN linear layer.

The data-format boundary transitions between INT8 and FP16 operations have been implemented correctly throughout the transformer block per Figure 6 and Section 4: (1) FP16 activations are quantized to INT8 immediately before each INT8 linear/BMM operator; (2) INT8 outputs from each GEMM are dequantized back to FP16 immediately after, before any FP16 element-wise operation; (3) no INT8 tensor is passed directly into a FP16 operation, and no FP16 tensor is passed directly into an INT8 GEMM without first being quantized. The dequantization applies the outer-dimension scale factors as in Equation 2 of Section 3.

The CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernel has been integrated as the backend for all INT8 linear module and BMM replacements described in Section 5.1: the implementation replaces the original FP16 torch.nn.Linear modules and bmm function calls with custom INT8 modules that invoke CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels. The kernels accept INT8 weight and activation tensors and return INT32 or FP16 outputs consistent with Equation 2 of Section 3 (outer-dimension FP16 scale application after the GEMM).

A visualization reproducing Figure 5 (Section 4) has been generated showing the effect of the SmoothQuant smoothing transformation with alpha = 0.5: before smoothing, the activation channels exhibit large outlier magnitudes (~100x larger than non-outlier channels) while weights are uniform; after smoothing with s_j = max(|X_j|)^0.5 / max(|W_j|)^0.5, both X_hat and W_hat show more balanced per-channel magnitudes, confirming that quantization difficulty is migrated from activations to weights and distributed more evenly.

The three SmoothQuant efficiency levels O1, O2, and O3 from Table 2 (Section 4) have been implemented as distinct quantization configurations: O1 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-token dynamic activation quantization; O2 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-tensor dynamic activation quantization; O3 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-tensor static activation quantization (scales computed offline from calibration).
The SmoothQuant O1 efficiency level from Table 2 (Section 4) has been implemented: O1 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-token dynamic activation quantization.
The SmoothQuant O2 efficiency level from Table 2 (Section 4) has been implemented: O2 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-tensor dynamic activation quantization.

The SmoothQuant O3 efficiency level from Table 2 (Section 4) has been implemented: O3 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-tensor static activation quantization (scales computed offline from calibration).
The experimental setup including baselines, models, datasets, evaluation harnesses, calibration procedure, and the O1-O3 quantization settings from Table 2 has been configured correctly.

All four baseline quantization schemes from Table 2 (Section 5.1) have been implemented and configured: (1) W8A8 naive — per-tensor weight and per-tensor dynamic activation; (2) ZeroQuant — group-wise weight and per-token dynamic activation; (3) LLM.int8() — per-channel weight and per-token dynamic activation with FP16 mixed-precision for outliers; (4) Outlier Suppression — per-tensor weight and per-tensor static activation. Each baseline must be runnable as a drop-in replacement for evaluation.

The W8A8 naive baseline from Table 2 (Section 5.1) has been implemented as a configurable module: weights use per-tensor INT8 quantization (a single scalar Δ = max(|W|) / 127 over the entire weight matrix) and activations use per-tensor dynamic INT8 quantization (a single scalar Δ computed from max(|X|) / 127 at runtime for each input activation tensor). The module is a drop-in replacement for FP16 nn.Linear and must produce the same output shape as the original layer.

The ZeroQuant baseline from Table 2 (Section 5.1) has been implemented as a configurable module: weights use group-wise INT8 quantization (a separate scale is computed per group of consecutive input channels, consistent with ZeroQuant Yao et al. 2022 and the definition in Figure 3 of Section 2) and activations use per-token dynamic INT8 quantization (a separate scale Δ_t = max(|X_t|) / 127 is computed at runtime for each token row of the activation matrix). The module is a drop-in replacement for evaluation.

The LLM.int8() baseline from Table 2 (Section 5.1) has been implemented as a configurable module following Dettmers et al. 2022: weights use per-channel INT8 quantization (a separate scale Δ_j = max(|W_j|) / 127 per input-channel row of W), activations use per-token dynamic INT8 quantization for non-outlier columns, and a mixed-precision decomposition keeps outlier activation columns in FP16 (identified at runtime by a magnitude threshold). The FP16 outlier sub-matrix and the INT8 non-outlier sub-matrix are computed separately and their results are summed to produce the final output. The module is a drop-in replacement for evaluation.

The Outlier Suppression baseline from Table 2 (Section 5.1) has been implemented as a configurable module following Wei et al. 2022: weights use per-tensor INT8 quantization (a single scalar Δ = max(|W|) / 127 over the entire weight matrix) and activations use per-tensor static INT8 quantization (a single scalar Δ pre-computed offline from calibration samples and fixed at inference time, not recomputed per input). The static scale is estimated from 512 Pile calibration sentences consistent with Section 5.1. The module is a drop-in replacement for evaluation.

Each of the four baseline quantization modules (W8A8 naive, ZeroQuant, LLM.int8(), Outlier Suppression) can be substituted as a drop-in replacement for the FP16 linear layers in OPT, BLOOM, and GLM-130B models and then run through the lm-evaluation-harness (for OPT/BLOOM) or GLM-130B's official evaluation repository (for GLM-130B) to produce per-task accuracy scores for comparison with Table 3 and Table 4. A single configuration flag or argument must select which baseline is active without requiring code changes.

The three SmoothQuant efficiency levels from Table 2 (Section 5.1) have been implemented as distinct runnable configurations: O1 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-token dynamic activation quantization; O2 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-tensor dynamic activation quantization; O3 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-tensor static activation quantization (scales pre-computed offline from calibration samples). The efficiency ordering O1 < O2 < O3 (lower latency) must be reflected in the implementation.

SmoothQuant-O2 has been implemented as a runnable configuration using per-tensor weight quantization and per-tensor dynamic activation quantization, as specified in Table 2 (Section 5.1).

SmoothQuant-O3 has been implemented as a runnable configuration using per-tensor weight quantization and per-tensor static activation quantization (scales pre-computed offline from calibration samples), as specified in Table 2 (Section 5.1). The efficiency ordering O1 < O2 < O3 (lower latency) is reflected in the implementation, consistent with Table 2 and Table 11 of Section 5.1 and 5.5 respectively, where coarser quantization granularity yields lower latency.

The calibration procedure from Section 5.1 has been implemented: 512 random sentences are sampled from the Pile pre-training dataset (Gao et al. 2020) validation set and used to estimate per-channel activation statistics (max(|X_j|) for j = 1..C_i) and static quantization step sizes (Δ) for each linear layer in the model. The same smoothed and quantized model derived from this one-time calibration pass is then applied unchanged for all downstream evaluation tasks without per-task re-calibration.

The grid search procedure for selecting the migration strength alpha described in Section 5.1 has been implemented: alpha is searched over a discrete grid (e.g., {0.1, 0.2, ..., 0.9, 1.0}) evaluated on a subset of the Pile validation set to identify the sweet-spot value per model family. The result is alpha = 0.5 for all OPT and BLOOM models, alpha = 0.75 for GLM-130B, alpha = 0.8 for LLaMA-1 models (Table 6), and model-specific values for Llama-2/Falcon/Mistral/Mixtral as given in Table 7.

The PyTorch Huggingface backend for SmoothQuant described in Section 5.1 has been implemented as a proof-of-concept: original FP16 torch.nn.Linear modules in the Huggingface model are replaced with custom INT8 linear modules that invoke CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels, and the bmm function in attention is replaced with an INT8 BMM implementation. This backend is used for single-GPU latency and memory benchmarks on OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, and OPT-30B (Figures 8 and Table 11) as stated in Section 5.3.

The FasterTransformer backend for SmoothQuant described in Section 5.1 has been implemented: SmoothQuant is integrated into the FasterTransformer serving framework, with INT8 linear modules and BMM operators using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels replacing the FP16 equivalents. The integration must support Tensor Parallelism (Shoeybi et al. 2019) for multi-GPU evaluation of OPT-13B, OPT-30B, OPT-66B, and OPT-175B as described in Section 5.3 (Figure 9, Table 10).

The GLM-130B-specific calibration adjustment described in Section 5.2 has been implemented: when calibrating the static quantization step sizes for GLM-130B, the top 2% of token activation values are clipped (following Wei et al. 2022) before computing the per-tensor static scale Δ. This clipping is applied only to GLM-130B (not to OPT or BLOOM models) and is required because GLM-130B has approximately 30% outlier channels making its activations more difficult to quantize.

The measured speedup and memory-saving results of SmoothQuant-O3 in PyTorch and FasterTransformer, including context stage, decoding stage, and the 530B single-node serving (Figures 8-9, Tables 8-10), have been reproduced.

The context-stage latency and memory benchmarks from Figure 8 (Section 5.3) using the PyTorch Huggingface backend have been reproduced: SmoothQuant-O3 is compared against FP16 baseline and LLM.int8() on OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, and OPT-30B (single GPU, A100 80GB) at batch size 4 with varying sequence lengths. SmoothQuant achieves up to 1.51x speedup on OPT-30B at sequence length 256, while LLM.int8() is almost always slower than FP16. Both SmoothQuant and LLM.int8() nearly halve the memory usage of the FP16 model, with SmoothQuant saving slightly more due to fully INT8 GEMMs.

The per-model speedup results from Figure 8 (Section 5.3) using the PyTorch Huggingface backend have been reproduced: SmoothQuant-O3 is consistently faster than the FP16 baseline on OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, and OPT-30B across all tested sequence lengths, achieving up to 1.51x speedup on OPT-30B at sequence length 256. The speedup trend shows that larger models benefit more from SmoothQuant quantization (the acceleration increases with model size), consistent with the description in Section 5.3.

Figure 8 (Section 5.3): SmoothQuant-O3 is consistently faster than the FP16 baseline on OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, and OPT-30B across all tested sequence lengths (e.g., 128, 256, 512) using the PyTorch Huggingface backend.

The per-model memory-saving results from Figure 8 (Section 5.3) using the PyTorch Huggingface backend have been reproduced: both SmoothQuant-O3 and LLM.int8() nearly halve the peak GPU memory usage of the FP16 baseline on OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, and OPT-30B. SmoothQuant-O3 saves slightly more memory than LLM.int8() because SmoothQuant uses fully INT8 GEMMs (no FP16 mixed-precision for outliers), consistent with the explanation in Section 5.3.

The LLM.int8() latency comparison result from Figure 8 (Section 5.3) using the PyTorch Huggingface backend has been reproduced: LLM.int8() is almost always slower than the FP16 baseline on OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, and OPT-30B across all tested sequence lengths, due to the large overhead of the mixed-precision activation representation (keeping outliers in FP16 while computing INT8 for non-outliers). This contrasts with SmoothQuant-O3, which is consistently faster than FP16 under the same benchmark conditions.

Notably, SmoothQuant achieves similar or better latency using half the number of GPUs (1 GPU instead of 2 for OPT-66B; 4 GPUs instead of 8 for OPT-175B). Memory is reduced by a factor of almost 2x as shown in Figure 9 bottom panel.

The benchmarking infrastructure for measuring end-to-end inference latency and peak GPU memory usage described in Section 5.3 has been implemented: (1) context-stage latency is measured as the time to generate all hidden states for a batch of 4 sentences in one forward pass; (2) peak GPU memory is recorded as the aggregated peak GPU memory during this pass; (3) decoding-stage latency is measured as the per-token latency during autoregressive generation; (4) all experiments are run on NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU servers; (5) the PyTorch Huggingface backend is used for single-GPU benchmarks (OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, OPT-30B) and the FasterTransformer backend with Tensor Parallelism is used for multi-GPU benchmarks (OPT-13B through OPT-175B and MT-NLG 530B).

The context-stage latency measurement has been implemented as described in Section 5.3: end-to-end inference latency is measured as the wall-clock time to generate all hidden states for a batch of exactly 4 sentences in a single forward pass (i.e., one complete context-stage forward pass through the entire LLM), using standard GPU timing primitives (e.g., CUDA events or torch.cuda.synchronize + time.perf_counter) to ensure accurate measurement without CPU-GPU synchronization artifacts.

The peak GPU memory measurement procedure has been implemented as described in Section 5.3: aggregated peak GPU memory usage (in GB) is recorded during each context-stage forward pass using torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() or equivalent, capturing the full peak footprint including model weights, activations, and any intermediate INT8 or FP16 buffers allocated during the pass. The memory counter is reset before each measurement run to avoid contamination from prior allocations.

The decoding-stage latency measurement has been implemented as described in Section 5.3: per-token autoregressive generation latency is measured as the average wall-clock time per generated token during the decoding (generation) stage, for OPT-30B (1 GPU) and OPT-175B (8 GPUs) across the four (batch size, sequence length) configurations in Table 8 — (BS=1, SeqLen=512), (BS=1, SeqLen=1024), (BS=16, SeqLen=512), (BS=16, SeqLen=1024). The measurement covers only the per-token autoregressive step, not the context-stage prefill.

All latency and memory benchmarks described in Section 5.3 are executed on NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU servers as specified in the paper. The hardware configuration must be documented (GPU model, GPU memory capacity, number of GPUs per node) and confirmed to be A100 80GB instances. Results obtained on other GPU architectures (e.g., V100, H100, A6000) do not satisfy this requirement, as throughput and memory capacity differ.

The PyTorch Huggingface backend is used exclusively for single-GPU benchmarks on OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, and OPT-30B, as stated in Section 5.3. The benchmark runner for the PyTorch backend does not enable Huggingface model parallelism (no device_map='auto' splitting across multiple GPUs), consistent with the paper's statement that 'due to the lack of support for model parallelism in Huggingface, we only measure SmoothQuant's performance on a single GPU for the PyTorch implementation.'

The FasterTransformer backend with Tensor Parallelism (Shoeybi et al. 2019) is used for multi-GPU benchmarks on OPT-13B, OPT-30B, OPT-66B, OPT-175B, and MT-NLG 530B, as described in Section 5.3. The Tensor Parallelism configuration must correctly partition the model weight matrices across the specified number of GPUs — specifically, 1 GPU for OPT-13B and OPT-30B, 2 GPUs (FP16) or 1 GPU (INT8) for OPT-66B, 8 GPUs (FP16) or 4 GPUs (INT8) for OPT-175B, and 16 GPUs (FP16) or 8 GPUs (INT8) for MT-NLG 530B — and the latency measurement must include the inter-GPU communication overhead from tensor parallelism.
The FasterTransformer backend with Tensor Parallelism is configured to run OPT-13B and OPT-30B on a single GPU for both FP16 and INT8 (SmoothQuant-O3) benchmarks, as described in Section 5.3.

The FasterTransformer backend with Tensor Parallelism is configured to run OPT-66B on 2 GPUs for FP16 and 1 GPU for INT8 (SmoothQuant-O3), as described in Section 5.3, with inter-GPU communication overhead included in latency measurement.

The FasterTransformer backend with Tensor Parallelism is configured to run OPT-175B on 8 GPUs for FP16 and 4 GPUs for INT8 (SmoothQuant-O3), as described in Section 5.3, with inter-GPU communication overhead included in latency measurement.

The FasterTransformer backend with Tensor Parallelism is configured to run MT-NLG 530B on 16 GPUs for FP16 and 8 GPUs for INT8 (SmoothQuant-O3), as described in Section 5.3, with inter-GPU communication overhead included in latency measurement.

The latency ordering across quantization schemes described in Section 5.5 has been verified from Table 11: for both OPT-13B and OPT-30B at both sequence lengths, the latency strictly decreases from O1 to O2 to O3 (coarser granularity yields lower latency), static quantization (O3) is faster than dynamic quantization (O1, O2) because runtime step-size computation is eliminated, and LLM.int8() is slower than FP16 while all SmoothQuant variants are faster than FP16.

The complete strict latency ordering across all five methods from Table 11 (Section 5.5) has been verified for all four (model, sequence length) combinations: the ordering LLM.int8() > FP16 > SmoothQuant-O1 > SmoothQuant-O2 > SmoothQuant-O3 must hold for OPT-13B at SeqLen=256 (237.1 > 152.6 > 124.5 > 120.5 > 112.1ms), OPT-13B at SeqLen=512 (371.5 > 296.3 > 243.3 > 235.1 > 223.1ms), OPT-30B at SeqLen=256 (387.9 > 343.0 > 246.7 > 240.2 > 227.6ms), and OPT-30B at SeqLen=512 (654.9 > 659.9 > 490.7 > 478.3 > 458.4ms). NOTE: For OPT-30B at SeqLen=512, LLM.int8()=654.9ms is slightly less than FP16=659.9ms — this is a potential discrepancy with the paper's claim that LLM.int8() is 'almost always' slower than FP16, with this being one exception. The ordering LLM.int8() ≈ FP16 for this specific case should be flagged.

The migration strength alpha ablation experiment for Figure 10 (Section 5.5) has been configured: SmoothQuant is applied to OPT-175B with alpha values swept across the range [0, 1] (e.g., {0.0, 0.1, 0.2, ..., 1.0}) using 512 Pile calibration sentences, and the resulting quantized model is evaluated on the LAMBADA zero-shot task using lm-evaluation-harness for each alpha value, producing a curve of LAMBADA accuracy versus alpha.

Figure 10 (Section 5.5) has been reproduced showing LAMBADA accuracy versus migration strength alpha on OPT-175B: when alpha is too small (< 0.4) the activations remain hard to quantize (LAMBADA accuracy collapses to near 0%); when alpha is too large (> 0.6) the weights become hard to quantize (LAMBADA accuracy also collapses); only the sweet-spot range alpha in [0.4, 0.6] yields small quantization errors for both weights and activations and maintains LAMBADA accuracy near the FP16 baseline (~74.7%). The optimal alpha = 0.5 lies in the center of this sweet-spot region.
